title: Keigo: Respectful and Humble Language
learningGoal: The learner will understand the dual system of Japanese Keigo (Honorifics), mastering the distinction between Sonkeigo (elevating others) and Kenjougo (lowering oneself).
instructions: In Japanese, being polite isn't just about adding 'desu' or 'masu'. To show true respect to a customer, boss, or stranger, you must use completely different verbs for their actions versus your own actions. This system is called Keigo.

lessonContent.bodyText: Japanese politeness is divided into three categories: 1) Teineigo (Polite language - the standard 'desu/masu' you already know). 2) Sonkeigo (Respectful language - raises the listener). 3) Kenjougo (Humble language - lowers the speaker).
Use these verbs only for the actions of someone you respect (a customer, a boss, a teacher). NEVER use them for yourself.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: いく・くる・いる -> いらっしゃる
たべる・のむ -> めしあがる
しゃちょうは いらっしゃいます。

lessonContent.bodyText: Use these verbs only for your own actions (or your in-group/family) when speaking to someone you respect. NEVER use them for the customer or boss.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: いく・くる -> まいる
たべる・のむ・もらう -> いただく
わたしが まいります。

lessonContent.bodyText: Here are the respectful and humble forms for 'Say' and 'Know'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: いう -> おっしゃる (尊敬) / もうす (謙譲)
しっている -> ごぞんじだ (尊敬) / ぞんじている (謙譲)

learningObjective: The learner will correctly select between Sonkeigo (respectful) and Kenjougo (humble) verbs based on the subject of the sentence, preventing critical social faux pas in business environments.

examples.targetText: きゃくは すしを めしあがりました。
examples.baseTranslation: The customer ate sushi. (Sonkeigo - Elevating the customer).
examples.explanation: If you say 'Watashi wa sushi wo meshiagarimashita', you are elevating yourself, which sounds arrogant.

examples.targetText: わたしは すしを いただきました。
examples.explanation: If you say 'Kyaku wa sushi wo itadakimashita', you are lowering the customer, which is incredibly rude.

importantRules.ruleText: NEVER use Sonkeigo for yourself. NEVER use Kenjougo for the listener or their out-group.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: This is the most strictly enforced rule in Japanese business communication.

importantRules.ruleText: When talking to a client about your own boss, you must use Kenjougo (humble language) for your boss.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: This is because your boss is in your 'in-group' relative to the client. You lower your entire company to respect the client.

cultureContext.contextText: Keigo is so complex that even native Japanese speakers take special training courses on it when they join a company. Mistakes in Keigo can cost business deals, as it shows a lack of social grace and respect.
dosList: Use Kenjougo (Mairimasu, Itashimasu) when introducing yourself to strangers or clients.
dontsList: Do not use Keigo with close friends or family. It will sound cold, distant, and sarcastic.

summary: Sonkeigo (Irasshaimasu, Meshiagarimasu) elevates the listener's actions. Kenjougo (Mairimasu, Itadakimasu) lowers the speaker's actions.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: If a customer asks if you will go to the meeting, do you reply with 'Mairimasu' or 'Irasshaimasu'?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: まいります (Mairimasu - Humble form for yourself)

checkpointQuestions.questionText: If you are asking the boss what they want to eat, which verb do you use?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: めしあがる (Meshiagaru - Respectful form for the boss)

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What is the humble form of 'To say' (Iu)?

keyTakeaways: Rule: Never respect yourself, never humble the client.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Mastering the Japanese business language hierarchy.
importantFacts: Irassharu covers go, come, and be. Mairu covers go and come.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learner must memorize the distinct vocabulary pairs for giving and receiving respect.

feedbackRules.onPass: Excellent! You understand the core concept of Keigo directionality.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review the difference between elevating (Sonkeigo) and lowering (Kenjougo).
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start. Focus on memorizing which verb belongs to which group. 'Meshiagaru' is for them, 'Itadaku' is for you.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: いらっしゃいます。しゃちょうは いらっしゃいます。まいります。わたしが まいります。めしあがる。いただく。おっしゃる。もうす。

commonMistakes: Saying 'Watashi wa irasshaimasu'. This is extremely arrogant.
